food_subgroup: Cooked prawn / shrimp dish with garlic seasoning

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for garlic prawns as a prepared dish; closest parent is shrimp food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code for garlic-seasoned butterfly prawns as a prepared dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 refers to prawn as a substance; no specific concept for garlic prawns as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of garlic prawns is approximately 250 g, comprising 4 to 6 large butterflied tiger prawns dressed with garlic sauce and garnished with spring onion.

nutrient_source.title: Prawns, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150) and adapted for garlic-sauce preparation using USDA Foundation Foods garlic and oil data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base prawn nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (NDB 15150: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat). Garlic, oil, soy sauce, and sesame oil contributions estimated using USDA Foundation Foods component data and standard recipe proportions for Chinese-style garlic prawns. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation. Values represent a composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for garlic prawns as a prepared dish. GI estimated at 5 based on the established principle that high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood dishes have negligible glycemic impact. Analogous reference: cooked shrimp and similar protein-dominant seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database consistently show GI values of 0–10. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (5 × 9.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.5, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced soy sauce, as a full restaurant serving of garlic prawns can contribute approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic index (~5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic prawns are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic index, making them unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; partially offset by omega-3 polyunsaturated fats and lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol content (~380 mg per serving) and high sodium load from soy sauce; however, the dish provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein, and moderate consumption as part of a heart-healthy diet is generally acceptable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Prawns (蒜蓉开边虾, Suàn Róng Kāi Biān Xiā) is a popular Chinese seafood dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants. The dish features large tiger prawns that are butterflied — split lengthwise along the back — and topped with a generous layer of minced garlic mixed with oil, soy sauce, and sometimes ginger or spring onion, then steamed or wok-cooked until the prawns are just cooked through and the garlic is fragrant and lightly golden. The result is a dish with a tender, juicy prawn texture, a savory-umami garlic aroma, and a mildly salty finish from the soy-based sauce. Nutritionally, garlic prawns are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 17.8 g protein and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a moderate fat content of 6.5 g largely from cooking oil. The dish is rich in selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and cholesterol, reflecting the nutritional profile of crustacean seafood. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is negligible (estimated ~5), making this dish highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is commonly ordered as a shared main course at Chinese zi char (煮炒) restaurants and is associated with celebratory or family dining occasions. The dish is halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients and is naturally dairy-free and gluten-free when tamari is substituted for soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic prawns?
Is garlic prawns suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat garlic prawns if I have high blood pressure?
Is garlic prawns high in cholesterol?
What are the nutrients in garlic prawns?
How is 蒜蓉开边虾 prepared?
meal_context: shared main course at Chinese zi char restaurants
seafood course in a multi-dish Chinese meal

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for garlic prawns as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data with estimated contributions from garlic, oil, and soy sauce seasoning based on standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked shrimp) is well-characterised in USDA data and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for cooked shrimp and is not reliably estimated for this preparation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible for vegetable-oil-based preparations of this type per USDA data. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked shrimp due to soy sauce seasoning typical of restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. The price of SGD 36.80 from field survey (Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant, Circular Road) reflects a full restaurant portion and is preserved from the input record for reference. Cuisine tag 'Roasted Duck' from the source platform is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data adapted for garlic-sauce preparation.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement.
Added Chinese traditional form (蒜蓉開邊蝦) in addition to simplified (蒜蓉开边虾) from input.
Estimated GI at 5 using analogous seafood evidence from Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN / University of Sydney GI Database.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed negligible in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all entries match verbatim.
Disregarded irrelevant 'Roasted Duck' cuisine tag from source platform.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI = 5 (≤ 55 threshold).
